There are two major types of human glial cells commonly transplanted into host animals: human astrocytes (including glial progenitor cells) and human oligodendrocyte progenitor cells. These transplanted cells can differentiate within the host animal: astrocyte precursors can mature into functional astrocytes, supporting neurons and homeostasis, while oligodendrocyte progenitor cells can develop into myelinating oligodendrocytes that wrap axons and promote efficient signal transmission.

Diabetic patients with acute coronary syndrome experience increased short-term and long-term risk for bleeding events. Several mechanisms contribute to the increased risk of bleeding events in diabetic patients with acute coronary syndrome (ACS). Diabetic patients often have platelet dysfunction, endothelial dysfunction, and are more likely to receive aggressive antithrombotic therapy due to their elevated risk for ischemic events. Additionally, diabetes-related comorbidities (such as renal impairment) further increase bleeding risk. As a result, diabetic patients with ACS experience increased rates of both short-term and long-term bleeding events compared to non-diabetic patients.

The center of a granuloma typically contains immune cells such as macrophages, which can differentiate into epithelioid cells and multinucleated giant cells. PDPN promotes efficient motility along stromal surfaces by activating the C-type lectin receptor to rearrange the actin cytoskeleton in dendritic cells. Podoplanin (PDPN) is a glycoprotein expressed on various cell types, including dendritic cells. PDPN promotes efficient motility of dendritic cells along stromal surfaces by activating C-type lectin receptors, which in turn triggers remodeling of the actin cytoskeleton. This cytoskeletal rearrangement facilitates dendritic cell movement, which is important for immune surveillance and response.
